title: 2025/09/11 - EDSI
displayTitle: 2025-09-11 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - Anti-Money Laundering and Countering Financing of Terrorism (Supervisor, Levy, and Other Matters) Amendment Bill
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: Anti-Money Laundering and Countering Financing of Terrorism (Supervisor, Levy, and Other Matters) Amendment Bill
description: Anti-Money Laundering and Countering Financing of Terrorism (Supervisor, Levy, and Other Matters) Amendment Bill Financial Services Federation Lyn McMorran, Executive Director Blind Low Vision Andrea Midgen, CEO Real Estate Institute of New Zealand Janine Tan, Senior Legal Advisor Lizzy Ryley, Chief Executive Cygnus Law Limited Simon Papa
descriptionSummary: Anti-Money Laundering and Countering Financing of Terrorism (Supervisor, Levy, and Other Matters) Amendment Bill. Financial Services Federation. Lyn McMorran, Executive Director

chunks.text: I think they're on Zoom, the first submitter. Yes. Um, they should be joining you now. Okay, thanks.

Good morning. Good morning. Uh, Lynn, can you hear me? I can, can you hear me? Yes, I can. We can hear you and we can see you as well, which is great. We have 10 minutes with you, so it's your time up to you how you want to use this time. Thank you. If you wanna speak to your submission, leave some time for questions up to you. Thank you. Um, So I'll say a few words and then leave some time for questions.

Thanks for that. So good morning and thank you for the opportunity for the Financial Services Federation to speak to our submission on this bill. Um, as you can see from our submission, the FSF is the industry body representing the specialist lending sector operating in New Zealand. And our members are commercial asset and leasing finance providers, motor vehicle financiers, credit unions, and building societies, specialist housing lenders, point of sale finance providers, et cetera.

And they have collectively 1.7 million New Zealand consumers and businesses who choose to be their customers. So whilst we're supportive of the bill's objectives, particularly with respect to reducing business compliance costs and improving regulatory effective effectiveness and efficiency, we are strongly opposed to the imposition of an industry levy to finance A-M-L-C-F-T regulatory activity.

Obviously, this is directly at odds with achieving the objective of reducing compliance costs. Frankly, we believe that placing the burden to fund A-M-L-C-F-T supervisory and enforcement activities on the businesses that are already paying to meet their obligations as the first line of defense against money laundering and financing of terrorism in New Zealand is outrageous.

As we've said in our written submission, we believe these activities should be funded directly from the proceeds of crime fund so that the criminals who are committing the offenses are the ones paying for the cost of compliance. We are therefore asking the committees, uh, that the committee seriously considers removing the requirement for industry to pay the levy to fund A-M-L-C-F-T regulatory activities and place this cost where it belongs with the criminals committing the crimes.

Secondly, the FSF is supportive of the proposal to move to a single A-M-L-C-F-T supervisor. Other larger jurisdictions such as Australia, have been able to manage their A-M-L-C-F-T regulatory activities with one supervisor and New Zealand should be able to do the same. A single supervisor will remove any possibility of confusion or mixed messages that can arise with three separate supervisors.

However, we do sound a note of caution with respect to the necessity of ensuring that the supervisor is adequately resourced and has the necessary expertise within its personnel to understand the very diverse sectors they will be supervising and the relative risks. Each of these pose are being used to launder money or finance terrorism to ensure that they don't adopt a one size fits all approach.

We also have quite significant concerns about the proposed extensions of powers to the DIA, a lot of which are at the discretion of the chief executive. Our concern with respect to this is that the DIA is not subject to the same governance oversight as the other current supervisors, the Reserve Bank and the FMA. This means in our view that the chief executive of the DIA will have almost unchecked par, which we believe is inappropriate.

This would include allowing a supervisor to issue binding codes, and we believe that mandatory consultation periods with affected entities must be a requirement for any such codes to ensure that they support practical compliance. There also needs to be an understanding on the part of the supervisor that many of the entities they will be supervising are very small businesses.

These do not necessarily have dedicated compliance teams like larger institutions do, and therefore may not be able to respond to urgent requests from the supervisor as quickly as they may wish. Finally, whilst we welcome the further clarification in the bill that address verification is not required for standard due diligence, we note that there's a similar provision in the current statute's amendment bill that is before the house.

It would be helpful for reporting entities to understand how the two bills will work together. Um, and it's also imperative that we just get on and remove the address verification requirement, particularly from the perspective of financial inclusion for people who are not easy, easily able to provide such evidence of their address.

So that's, that's the main, um, points of our submission and I'm happy to take any questions, Members, any questions? All right. Uh, Lynn, I have a question. So, um, you are opposing the, the levy. Um, what would you say to the view of those who think that, uh, entities that benefit from a safe financial system should be contributing towards maintaining it as well?

What would you say to that view? That's a really good question. Thank you for asking. I think, um, the, the fact is they already are, um, they are already paying for that compliance through having, um, to have the compliance within their business of, um, a proper risk assessment of, of their, of their own particular risk of, um, being used for laundering money or finance and terrorism.

They have to have processes in place. They have to have, uh, regular audits of their process, which is a, um, from an independent, um, body, which is, which is a very expensive exercise, they have to have, um, a ML offices. They have to have, uh, appropriate reporting to their board and governance structure.

They are already paying to be the first line of defense against money laundering and countering fi financing of terrorism to then have to pay on top of that a levy to be supervised, um, when they are not. The problem just seems, um, in, in my view and the view of our members to be disproportionate and unfair.

Just another question on that. So, uh, cost of audits and the reporting that the requirements, uh, don't you think that is like, that comes with the nature of the business, that that is like the basic require requirement you would expect from these businesses? Yes. Yeah, I would agree with that. And, and, and they're doing it, um, and have been doing it.

Um, our members have been part of the A ML, uh, regime right from the very beginning. So they have worn their, the compliance cost for a number of years already. Um, and they're already doing it. And, you know, they do understand that there is the possibility that they could be used to find to, to, uh, launder money. Um, so they take that very seriously.

They take the, the reporting of suspicious transactions or suspicious, suspicious activity very seriously. We do a lot of training with our members, um, on updating them on what's happening with a ML, any changes to the regime. Um, we get the supervisors in, um, we get a ML experts in to talk to our members about trends that they're seeing, risks to look out for, all of that sort of thing.

So they're, they're already paying for, um, very strong compliance and they believe in and, and very strong compliance and, and understand the need for it. It's this extra step in this extra cost of imposing the levy on the already compliant people who are already doing their best to prevent a ML and, and, uh, prevent money laundering and terrorism financing that they're objecting to.

Alright. We have a question now from Dr. David Wilson. Rudy Lynn. Um, thank you very much. Yeah, you're very good. Uh, submission, um, you mentioned that you could be in favor of a single A MLC supervisor if that were to be, so what would your choice be? Um, well, I do think the DIA already has a very broad range of supervisory activity.

The, the RBN Z's very much just focused on banks and non-bank deposit takers. Um, and the FMA focuses on the financial advisors. So it does make sense that the DIA become the, the supervisor. The concern is around just, um, ensuring that the chief executive of the DIA doesn't have completely unchecked par because the DIA has a different governance structure to, to that of the RBNZ and the FMA, I mean, they will be taking on a lot of extra work, um, because the banks are large and, and, um, and you know, the non-bank deposit takers are not,

of course, uh, they're quite small, but the banks are extremely large in that that will create a lot more work for them. So I think our point was about making sure that they were adequately resourced and, and I think, you know, it would make sense to me to be considering how they can bring people across from RBNZ and FMA who are experts in the A ML supervision of their particular fields, how they can bring those people across to be part of their team and cross pollinate that, that, um, that knowledge and experience.
Thank you. Thank you so much, Lynn, for your submission and for your time. Thank you. Bye-bye. Thank you. You're welcome. Thanks very much. Very much. So, next we have blind low vision.
Good morning, Andrea,

Can you hear me? Yes, we can hear you. That's good. We have 10 minutes with you. Thank you. After you how you want to use this time, if you want to speak to your submission, leave some time for questions. Up to you. Thank you. Thank you. Um, so my name is Andrea Gin. I'm the CEO of Blind Vision, uh, New Zealand we're the operating name of the Royal New Zealand Foundation and the Blind and our organization has been, uh, around for 130 years.

Um, we exist to empower approximately 16,000 clients across the motto who are blind, deaf, blind or have low vision to live the life they choose. We estimate that 193,000 New Zealand does live with vision loss and that's expect, um, to, to grow to 225,000 by 2028.

Some of the common eye conditions include age related macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy, glaucoma, and cataracts. And this is very indicative of our aging population for the growth. Um, B-L-B-N-Z is a recipient of both funding grants and gifts from wills.

Um, the work of, uh, B-L-B-N-Z has for a great many years substantially depended on the goodwill of New Zealand society and private donations both into Veeva. And it's not uncommon for many people to make financial provision to blv and Z in their wills and gifts from an estate can arrange in value anywhere between low hundreds of dollars to multimillions.

Our data shows that 40% to 70% of our donations come from gifts left and wills. The percent depends on the size of the gift that has been willed. It's important that both the person who has left the gift as well as the family and loved ones, that they have confidence that the benefactor receives what was intended.

So in terms of our context of our submission, we support the need for a single supervisor as proposing the bill. However, there needs to be further clarity and consistency in the way the requirements are interpreted and also implied to the intermediaries. The complexity and variation in a ML requirements can constitute unnecessary pressures on recipient organizations.

There are significant variations in what different organizations require in terms of a ML and some of which are time consuming and incur unnecessary costs for charities, which is not in keeping with the explanatory note for this bill. Uh, and these consistencies result in, um, increased administrative and volunteer time costs.

Charities must spend limited staff and volunteer capacity on compliance instead of delivering services. And this can result in fewer programs being run reduced support to beneficiaries, um, and our clients and greater burnout among already stretched teams. Um, there's also disproportionate compliance burdens.

So regulatory obligations that do not match the actual risk profile can discourage donors and executors, um, potentially leading to fewer bequests being realized. And for charities, this means lost opportunities to fund long-term projects and reduce confidence in their ability to steward gifts effectively. Um, registered charities are already required to identify the governing trustee or members under the charities Act 2005 with a ML requirements, thus duplicating a level of formal disclosure.

So in conclusion, we require clarity within the drafted bill, as well as any regulations that provide clarity and consistency in the way the requirements are interpreted and applied by intermediaries. Um, being able to live to gift and will to blind low vision is not just about honoring those wishes of the departed loved ones, but also to honor those who receive support.

Um, blind low vision can only continue to build on the goodwill of New Zealanders and private donations of the rules and regulations can be easily understood and complied with. Thank you. That's good. Um, yeah, so I am looking around for any questions. No, um, Andrea, I'll ask one question.

So how is the compliance burden looking for you currently? Uh, well, like all compliance that it's a, it's a necessary, um, thing to do, but, uh, again, as I said earlier, with stretch resources, I mean, our organization is there to provide services. Um, the impact as all these compliance costs go up is that we have to reduce services, and that will actually in the end, um, put a burden on the government to, to support the blind, deaf, blind and, and low vision community.
Thank you so much, Andrea, for your submission. Thank you for your time. Kira,

Have the, we have a submitter after that, so Yep. We, If Andrea is still there. I have a question for Andrew. Yes, Andrea, are you still there? I am. Oh, that's great. So, uh, we have a question from Dr. David Wilson, if you don't mind answering that. No. Yeah. Hi Andrea. Thank you very much for your submission.

Um, just a little bit interested in your views on the restricted, uh, distributions in excess of $50,000 and what you've said today. Um, flags of red. Well, it gives me a red flag in terms of the fact that, um, much of the anti-money laundering happens in receiving banks, much of the money laundering.

So there's quite a lot of compliance at both ends, and you are not, and you are talking about being able to verify that the benefactor receives what was intended. Um, so we have a pinch point, don't we? Yep, absolutely. You Make comment on that for us, please. Yeah, I mean, we, we understand why, um, the necessity for it all, but it is impacting on us to be able to receive something that someone's simply left in their will and not expecting us to bear the burden of, um, cost to be able to realize that gift.
Okay. So the 50 thousand's, like an arbitrary amount that's imposed on you, correct? Yeah. Thank you. Okay, thank you Andrea, Kara. Yep. Bye-bye. Alright, so we have, uh, Cignas Law Limited. Thanks.

I think I saw Simon online before, but he's disappeared. Okay. Yeah, he was there. Yeah. Good morning, Simon. Uh, good morning. Can you hear me? We can hear you and we can see you as well. We have 10 minutes with you. Great. Thank you for the opportunity to make a submission.

Uh, so I am a lawyer and I have a law firm, CIS Law. So Cigna's Law is subject to the A-M-L-C-F-T regime as a reporting entity and also advisors clients on compliance with the regime. Um, so Cignas law submission boils down to one key point, and that's the proposed delegation of rulemaking powers to the chief executive of the Department of Internal Affairs, who will be the effective person and to the commissioner of police should not be approved by the committee.

That's because the Ministry of Justice and the A ML supervisors have shown, in my view, an overwhelming interest in increasing regulation regardless of the costs or benefits. At the same time, they've shown little interest and measures to address unnecessary A ML regulation, even with the government's focus on reducing UN unnecessary A ML regulation.

The four current bills making changes to the A-M-L-C-F-T Act are still overwhelmingly focused on MA matters other than deregulation. And this current bill is an example of that. The initiative to remove unnecessary a ML regulation has been championed by the parties making up the governing coalition, not by the Ministry of Justice or any government agencies.

I appreciate that the delegation of rule making powers will have a moderate impact on if timeliness of of rulemaking. However, remind you, the overwhelming focus on officials is on increasing regulation. So it's very likely that the rulemaking powers will be used to impose even more a ML obligations on reporting entities without adequate consideration of the costs and benefits of doing that, that negative outcome will outweigh any efficiency gains.

And in any case, the current system has already resulted in dozens of new regulations being imposed over the last three years. So the, the current regime seems quite capable of, um, churning out lots of rules. So I submit that the power make rules on a ML matters are retained by the executive, and that the existing delegated powers in relation to a ML matters are retained by the Minister of Justice.

Um, on a slightly diff different topic, but related, um, I've noted in Cignas law's submission, my view that the implementation of the A ML regulatory regime has been somewhat chaotic, which is another reason to be cautious about devolving rulemaking powers in the schedule to the submission, I've listed the 60 different documents issued by government agencies and the police that are relevant to Cignas Law as a reporting entity.

Many of them are legally binding to various extents. They're presented as a confetti of guidelines, fact sheets, FAQs statements, and codes of practice. Most are individual PDF documents. There is no central register of those documents. There is no system for advising when they're issued or updated.

There is no system for advising when they're canceled and none has ever been issued with an implementation period that would actually give reporting entities time to comply before they come into force. So in my view, on any measure, the the information and document management of government agencies in relation to these matters has been somewhat lacking.

So regardless of the committee's view on the proposed rulemaking powers, I would urge the committee to require that the bill be updated to mandate that those documents are issued and managed in a much more effective manner to assist. So you can, that would assist reporting entities and better complying with the regime and also allow 'em to focus more on matters other than trying to keep up to date with what documents have been issued and updated.

Another key consideration in relation to the PDF documents is that they're not inherently readable by generative AI tools such as Chat, GPT and Google's AI overview. The increasing use of AI software as a primary tool for obtaining information from the internet means that many people, uh, will miss relevant law and guidance set out on PDF documents.

So the practice of using PDF documents as a method for publishing a ML rules and guides should not be supported. So at the very least, I submit the bill require that all rules and other outcomes of the exercise, of delegated powers be published in hyper text markup language and that they'd be published on one webpage so that they're much easier to find and access.

So thank you. That's a, a brief summary of my submissions. I'd be happy to answer questions if there are any. Thank you, Simon. And we have a question from Dr. Uh, thank you Madam Chair, and thank you for your submission and um, that very practical, uh, advice. It's very useful to hear that kind of thing and to see all of those, uh, separate pieces of law just pertaining to this one aspect, um, makes me wonder, uh, how many other, uh, lists like this must there be, um, for various different, um, elements of the law that you have to deal with.

And, um, I guess, have you ever, have you made any submissions to, um, any of our, um, digitizing government or anything else like that? Uh, no, I haven't. I just don't have the, the time to do that. Yeah, I'm aware of those, uh, of, of that work being undertaken.

I dunno what to what extent it will apply to all of the matters I've pointed out. Mm-hmm. Um, so it'd be great if that could, that that could be a tool to address that. But, um, I, I just wonder whether there's an opportunity in this bill to at least prompt or set some minimum standards regardless.

For example, as I said, requiring that, uh, everything be published A as HTML rather than as PDF, so that at least it's machine readable in the first instance. Okay. Thank you very much. That's good. Thank you someone, Simon. Thank you. Thank you. Alright.
Um, so do we have, um, real Estate Institute? Oh, thank you.

Good morning. Morning. Can you hear me okay? Yes, we can hear you and we can see you as well. Is that Janine and Lizzie? Yes, yes. Thank you. We have 10 minutes with you. Thank you. Yes, yes. Thank you Chair and uh, select committee members for the opportunity to make our oral submissions today.

On behalf of the Real Estate Institute of New Zealand, uh, as you said, I'm Lizzie Riley, I'm the chief executive, and with me I've got Melissa BA here and I've also actually got Janine Tan as well. So, uh, Ryan, you probably know Ryan's as a membership organization representing approximate approximately 95% of the real estate sector across New Zealand, where are re we represent all of the sectors of real estate from residential to commercial and industrial sales and leasing to business brokering.

Um, and overall we support approximately 17, 17,000 real estate professionals. So advocating on behalf of our members is a very important issue, and we focus on the issues that impact them the most. Um, we welcome the opportunity to highlight our members' concerns about this A-M-L-C-F-T amendment bill.

The real estate sector is highly compliant and A-M-L-C-F-T compliance costs are already disproportionate for our sector. Our members are very, very concerned by this proposed new industry levy. Years back, our members estimated the cost of a ML compliance collectively at around $30 million, and we expect that this would be far greater.

Now, some members tell us on a daily basis that they spend hundreds of thousands of dollars every year in both direct costs and the time of their teams. Just to keep up with the A ML compliance, the real estate sector should definitely be excluded from this proposed new levy for the reasons which I'll let Melissa go into further.

Good morning. So the vast majority of real estate agencies are small to medium sized businesses, and our members tell us how incredibly challenging it is for them to meet their obligations while still running viable agencies. So many operate on really tight margins and they just can't absorb another cost.

So an additional levy is likely in many or most cases to be passed on to consumers, and this is likely to occur by a way of a higher administrative fee charged to a vendor. So since phase two of the regime came into effect in 2019, our members have made major investments.

So this is things like customer due diligence, um, training staff nationwide, upgrading systems and processes, annual reporting, ongoing audits, and for small to medium sized business, small to medium sized agencies. This is a really heavy burden and it's disproportionate to the level of risk posed by the real estate agency work that's captured.

I think it's also important to note the risk profile of our sector is materially lower than high risk sectors such as banking and finance. So our sector is very compliant. Um, it takes our, our sector takes their a ML obligations very seriously. So the national risk assessment last year confirmed the real estate sector's risk profile has remained unchanged since 2019.

So a risk-based system, which we know is what is intended, should focus resources and levy costs on higher risk sectors where more work is required. So, for instance, the real estate sector is not a large filer of PTs or prescribed transaction reports.

By contrast, the higher risk sectors generate significantly higher workload for the supervisor. So the unintended consequence of this new levy is likely to be additional costs being passed on to everyday kiwis buying or selling a home. They're going to pay more, not because real estate sales people are earning more, but 'cause of additional regulatory costs.

So in summary, we strongly advocate for the real estate sector to be excluded from this new levy altogether. Our members are already shouldering a disproportionate compliance burden and imposing an additional levy as unjustified and risks unintended consequences impacting consumers.

So in the event our sector is not excluded from the levy, then in our view, the levy framework has to be fair, transparent, proportionate, truly risk-based, and there needs to be broad consultation with our sector, and the consultation has to be mandatory.

Um, it's important to note the real estate sector is very diverse. So you've got small family businesses, you've got large family businesses, you've got corporates with extensive national operations. There's no sort of one size fits all approach that will work here. And lastly, the cost of supervising a high risk sector like banking cannot be subsidized by lower risk sectors like real estate.

Those are kind of the main points we wanted to reiterate. Do you have questions for us? So, um, do you, um, I suggest you finish speaking to your submission. Have you finished speaking? Do you have a few more points you said? Uh, no, no, those are the main points we wanted to make. Oh, okay. Thank you. Uh, any questions members?

Um, so there are no questions here. There's a, oh, there is a question from Dr. David Wilson. Thank you. Um, Thank you for your submission. Um, can you tell me if, uh, what percentage of your transactions go through banks? We don't have the data with us, like as in, sorry, we don't have the information with us at the moment on how it goes through banks.

And that's why we think that we will need a target consultation for the industry to better know how the, how this proposed level will impact the day-to-day business operations of the real estate agency. So, um, with that in mind, which area do you think, um, is the highest risk in terms in terms of money laundering?

Which sector? Where's the pinch point? Banking and finance sector? I mean, also you need to bear in mind that you've got real estate agents who are doing CDD and then you've got banks and you've got solicitors and you've got accountants doing CDD on those exact same parties and they have more visibility of the transaction.

The MRA, the 2024 NRA, the National Risk Assessment has also highlighted the high risk sectors, which are the banks, the money remitters and the virtual asset providers, uh, virtual asset service providers. So these will be the high risk sectors that we think that the government should focus their resources, um, on.
Thank you. All right, that's good. Thank you so much for your submission. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you. Bye-bye.
Alright, um, so.
